hallucinations.description: The paper reports a successful H-M1 granularity experiment with nonzero repair rates and significant ANOVA results, but the provided code/log evidence shows an initial full H-M1 run where all five conditions had 0/304 successes and ANOVA was undefined (NaN). Because the paper claims definitive experimental findings without reconciling that contradictory run, the reported results are not reliably supported by the experiment record as presented.
hallucinations.evidence:
Paper: "One-way ANOVA indicates that granularity has a statistically significant effect on repair success (F=23.89, p=3.5e-19, eta-squared=0.059)." Code/log: in `experiment.log` for H-M1, "G0: 0/304 = 0.00% ... G4: 0/304 = 0.00%" and "F-statistic: nan
  p-value: nan
  eta-squared: 0.0000
  Gate passed: False".

hallucinations.description: The paper states that a one-way ANOVA was used on a within-subject design where the same 304 cases were evaluated under all five granularity conditions. Standard one-way ANOVA assumes independent groups, which is violated here because outcomes are repeated measures on the same cases. A repeated-measures analysis (or methods tailored to paired binary data) would be appropriate instead. Thus the reported ANOVA methodology and inference are mathematically invalid for the stated design.
hallucinations.evidence: Paper: "A within-subject design was employed where the same 304 runtime error cases were repaired under all five granularity conditions." and "Primary analysis: One-way ANOVA across five granularity levels".

hallucinations.description: The reported McNemar chi-squared statistic for G0 vs G3 is inconsistent with the contingency table. With discordant counts b=77 and c=1, the exact McNemar p-value may be tiny, but the continuity-corrected chi-squared would be about 72.1 and the uncorrected chi-squared about 73.2, not 77. The paper appears to confuse the discordant count with the test statistic.
hallucinations.evidence: Paper: "McNemar chi-squared | 77" alongside contingency table "G0 Success/G3 Failure = 77" and "G0 Failure/G3 Success = 1".

hallucinations.description: Multiple cited works appear nonexistent, future-dated, or mismatched to the claimed venue/arXiv record. These references cannot be verified from the information given and are likely fabricated or hallucinated.
hallucinations.evidence: Examples from References: "Huang, Y. et al. (2026). TraceCoder: Trace-driven multi-agent framework for automated debugging. arXiv preprint arXiv:2602.06875."; "Kong, W. et al. (2025). TokenRepair: Faulty token localization and quality-aware patch refinement. arXiv preprint arXiv:2511.18001."; "Dikici, A. and Bilgin, Z. (2025). Automated program repair: A survey. arXiv preprint."; "Huang, Y. et al. (2025). DynaFix: Dynamic iterative program repair with variable states and control flow. arXiv preprint."; "Huang et al., 2026" is also cited in the text as prior work.

hallucinations.description: The paper claims prior work includes "reinforcement learning from execution feedback (Gehring et al., 2024)" and cites it as an ICML paper. However, the reference list gives "RLEF: Grounding code LLMs in execution feedback with reinforcement learning" with authors and venue details that are not supported elsewhere in the provided materials and appear fabricated. This misstates the research landscape and methodology of prior work.
hallucinations.evidence: Paper text: "Subsequent work has extended this approach with ... reinforcement learning from execution feedback (Gehring et al., 2024)." References: "Gehring, J., Zheng, K., Copet, J., Mella, V., Cohen, T., and Synnaeve, G. (2024). RLEF: Grounding code LLMs in execution feedback with reinforcement learning. In International Conference on Machine Learning."

overall_assessment: Hallucinations were found. The most serious issues are unsupported/contradictory experimental claims and invalid statistical analysis for the stated within-subject design. The paper also contains likely fabricated citations and at least one inconsistent reported test statistic. Overall severity is substantial.
